primary_diagnosis.name: Infected residual-limb (stump) ulcer with early surrounding cellulitis and an acute phantom limb pain flare in a trans-tibial amputee

pathophysiology_summary: Repetitive prosthetic friction on the residual limb produced contact dermatitis and then a breach in the skin (superficial ulceration). In an amputated limb — a compromised host with altered perfusion, scar tissue and reduced protective sensation — bacterial entry through that breach seeds early cellulitis (localised erythema, warmth, tenderness) with a low-grade systemic response (37.6 C). Untreated or under-treated, this can progress to spreading cellulitis, abscess, or osteomyelitis of the residual bone, risking hospitalisation or revision amputation. Superimposed is an acute neuropathic pain crisis: mechanical irritation of a residual stump neuroma plus a central/peripheral-sensitisation-driven phantom limb pain flare, both refractory to the patient's exhausted PRN analgesia. The absence of crepitus, fluctuance, high fever, confusion or rapidly ascending erythema argues against necrotising infection or established deep sepsis at this moment, but the compromised-limb substrate mandates hands-on assessment because depth of infection and osteomyelitis cannot be excluded clinically, let alone remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Infected stump ulcer / early cellulitis of the residual limb
differential.evidence_basis: New skin breakdown with surrounding erythema and warmth in a compromised limb, rapidly worsening over 4 hours.

differential.diagnosis: Acute phantom limb pain flare / neuropathic pain crisis
differential.evidence_basis: Severe burning, cramping pain in the missing limb, unrelieved by exhausted usual analgesia — but does not explain the skin breakdown or erythema.
Exquisite focal point tenderness at one spot; contributes to pain but is not the acute infective threat.
Unilateral limb pain always prompts VTE screening; here no calf swelling, calf tenderness or engorged veins in the contralateral limb.
A compromised-limb wound can seed bone infection; superficial ulcer, short duration and no systemic toxicity make it less likely now but it cannot be excluded remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the phantom-pain narrative is loud and the infection is quiet — the key reasoning move is to NOT let the phantom pain overshadow the new skin breakdown.

differential.diagnosis: Infected stump ulcer with early cellulitis
differential.evidence_basis: Skin breach + surrounding erythema/warmth + low-grade temp, no systemic toxicity — early localised infection in a compromised limb.
Cannot be excluded without examination and imaging; keeps the disposition at same-day hands-on assessment rather than remote treatment.
Always screen for it with limb infection; absence of crepitus, fluctuance, pain out of proportion with dusky skin, and no systemic toxicity argue against it now.
No calf swelling, calf tenderness or superficial venous engorgement; presentation is a skin/wound problem, not a venous one.
Pain is localised to the neuroma and mechanical friction site without diffuse autonomic or trophic skin change — CRPS is unlikely to be the acute driver.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the systemic-sepsis features are negative, which correctly steps the disposition DOWN from ED/000 to same-day in-person assessment — but NOT down to routine GP, because the compromised-limb infection and osteomyelitis risk remain.

differential.diagnosis: Infected residual-limb ulcer with early cellulitis + acute phantom limb pain flare
differential.evidence_basis: Best fit for the whole picture; requires same-day in-person wound examination, swab, empirical antibiotics, neuropathic-pain optimisation and strict prosthetic rest, with a sepsis safety-net.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-state is recognition of a treatable acute infection behind a phantom-pain story, escalated to urgent same-day in-person care — neither dismissed nor over-escalated to 000 in the absence of systemic features.

red_flags.description: Skin breakdown / open ulceration in a compromised (amputated) limb — a portal for infection.
red_flags.clinical_significance: A breach in the skin of a limb with altered perfusion and reduced protective sensation lets infection establish and progress quickly.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive cellulitis, abscess, osteomyelitis, hospitalisation, or revision amputation.

red_flags.description: Surrounding erythema and warmth larger than the ulcer — early cellulitis.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates active soft-tissue infection needing examination, swab and empirical antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Spreading cellulitis and systemic sepsis if left untreated.

red_flags.description: Uncontrolled neuropathic pain with the patient's usual analgesia exhausted — acute pain crisis.
red_flags.clinical_significance: A pain crisis both distresses the patient and increases fall risk; needs same-day analgesic review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrelieved suffering, falls, and loss of function.

red_flags.description: Inability to bear weight plus environmental trip hazards — high falls risk.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Falls in an older amputee can cause fracture or head injury.

red_flags.description: Systemic-sepsis features — fever >=38.5 C, rapidly ascending erythema, purulent discharge, or confusion.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their appearance converts this from an urgent same-day problem to a life-threatening emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the whole presentation to 'just phantom pain / part of being an amputee' and missing the new infected ulcer — the central trap of this case.
Treating the loud neuropathic pain flare as the only problem and under-treating the quieter but more dangerous infection.
Prescribing antibiotics or giving definitive reassurance without arranging hands-on examination of a compromised-limb wound whose depth cannot be judged remotely.
Dismissing 37.6 C as trivial — in a compromised host with a fresh wound, even a low-grade temperature is a meaningful early-infection signal.
Sending straight to 000/ED when there are no systemic features — the correct level is urgent same-day in-person assessment, not an ambulance.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Depth of the ulcer and whether it probes to bone
Presence of fluctuance, abscess or crepitus
True extent and border of the erythema (needs marking and inspection)
Accurate temperature, blood pressure and heart rate without validated devices
Wound swab / microbiology and inflammatory bloods (FBC, CRP)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (same-day GP, urgent care, or hospital-in-the-home) for wound examination, swab and antibiotic prescription and analgesic optimisation; hold a clear ED/000 safety-net for systemic features. The AI Doctor cannot examine the wound or prescribe antibiotics remotely.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The dominant risk is UNDER-action through diagnostic overshadowing — treating this as a routine phantom-pain flare and missing an infected compromised-limb wound that can progress to osteomyelitis.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic — Cellulitis and erysipelas
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Empirical oral antistaphylococcal/antistreptococcal cover (e.g. cephalexin or di/flucloxacillin) for mild non-purulent cellulitis, with review; escalate for systemic features or failure to improve.
Any residual-limb skin breakdown warrants prosthetic rest and prompt clinical review; friction ulcers in a compromised limb are treated as higher-risk than intact-skin equivalents.
